other_observers: Seen with Lisa Edwards, Maggie Boswell, and Cole Wild; Found by Brandon Percival, Mark Peterson, and Brad and Brian Steger at Lake Holbrook, Otero County on 12 Sept. Also seen by others at Lake Meredith on 12 Sept., including Glenn Walbek, who got diagnostic photos.  Independently seen by Sco
specific_location: Seen from SWA on North Side of Lake Meredith (at end of County Lane 21.)
description: A large tern with strikingly dark upperparts and mostly white underparts. Entire dorsal surface (body feathers, crown, and upper wing and tail surfaces) appeared black with the exception of small white forecrown area. When banking the tail was seen to be deeply forked. If the tail had white edging it went unobserved at the distance and optical conditions I was operating under. The bill appeared long and all dark. A dark line extended from the bill through the eye, separating the white forecrown from the white cheeks and throat.  The underparts appeared to be essentially white including undertail, vent, belly, and throat, with white extending up to the cheek. The legs, tucked in flight, were unobserved. The wing lining was white, with a dark (almost black) trailing edge and broad black wingtips. The underside of the tail was not observed from my observing angle as I saw the bird from the side, from behind, and banking away, showing its dorsal aspects.  The wing was habitually held with a pronounced kink at the wrist, more distinctly exhibited than on other terns I've observed.Behaviors: The bird was seen in flight twice, foraging on Lake Meredith. I saw it drop to the surface once, after which it flew to a gull/pelican/tern roost across the lake and landed, lost in the crowd and air distortion.Call: not heardPlumage: Adult
